metadata.title: What You Can't See Is Killing You: Invisible Design in Games
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.

tags: What You Can't See Is Killing You: Invis
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. A horror game runs a continuous sensory simulation rather than just playing occasional scary sounds. Footstep variants are designed so you can never fully resolve what surface you are on. That unresolved ambiguity keeps the brain in a state of vigilance and threat detection. The evolutionary logic is plain: unexplained sounds once signaled real danger worth fearing.

artifactLines: A horror game runs a continuous sensory simulation rather than just playing occasional scary sounds.
Footstep variants are designed so you can never fully resolve what surface you are on.
That unresolved ambiguity keeps the brain in a state of vigilance and threat detection.
The evolutionary logic is plain: unexplained sounds once signaled real danger worth fearing.

beats.narration_text: The idea that a horror game runs a continuous sensory simulation, with each footstep variant preserving uncertainty about the surface underfoot, genuinely unsettled me. I want to understand why my brain treats ambiguous audio as threat. Break it down: (1) the evolutionary reason unresolved sound cues trigger vigilance rather than calm; (2) how designers engineer footstep variation so I never fully classify a surface; (3) a concrete test I could run on a horror game to detect this manipulation in action.
